question:
Let $S = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ for some integer $n > 1$. Say a permutation
$\pi$ of $S$ has a \emph{local maximum} at $k \in S$ if
\begin{enumerate} % The for parts are aligned in the AMM version
\item[(i)]
$\pi(k) > \pi(k+1)$ for $k=1$;
\item[(ii)]
$\pi(k-1) < \pi(k)$ and $\pi(k) > \pi(k+1)$ for $1 < k < n$;
\item[(iii)]
$\pi(k-1) < \pi(k)$ for $k=n$.
\end{enumerate}
(For example, if $n=5$ and $\pi$ takes values at $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ of
$2, 1, 4, 5, 3$, then $\pi$ has a local maximum of 2 at $k=1$,
and a local maximum of 5 at $k=4$.)
What is the average number of local maxima of a permutation of $S$,
averaging over all permutations of $S$?
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By the linearity of expectation, the average number of local maxima is equal
to the sum of the probability of having a local maximum at $k$ over
$k=1,\dots, n$.
For $k=1$, this probability is 1/2: given the pair
$\{\pi(1), \pi(2)\}$, it is equally likely that $\pi(1)$ or $\pi(2)$ is
bigger. Similarly, for $k=n$, the probability is 1/2. For $1 < k < n$,
the probability is 1/3: given the pair $\{\pi(k-1), \pi(k), \pi(k+1)\}$,
it is equally likely that any of the three is the largest.
Thus the average number of local maxima is
\[
2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + (n-2) \cdot \frac{1}{3} =
\frac{n+1}{3}.
\]

\textbf{Second solution:}
Another way to apply the linearity of expectation is to compute the
probability that $i \in \{1, \dots, n\}$ occurs as a local maximum.
The most efficient way to do this is to imagine the permutation
as consisting of the symbols $1, \dots, n, *$ written in a circle in
some order. The number $i$ occurs as a local maximum if the two symbols
it is adjacent to both belong to the set $\{*, 1, \dots, i-1\}$. There are
$i(i-1)$ pairs of such symbols and $n(n-1)$ pairs in total, so the
probability of $i$ occurring as a local maximum is $i(i-1)/(n(n-1))$, and
the average number of local maxima is
\begin{align*}
\sum_{i=1}^n \frac{i(i-1)}{n(n-1)} &=
\frac{2}{n(n-1)} \sum_{i=1}^n \binom{i}{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{n(n-1)} \binom{n+1}{3} \\
&= \frac{n+1}{3}.
\end{align*}
One can obtain a similar (if slightly more intricate)
solution inductively, by removing the known
local maximum $n$ and splitting into two shorter sequences.

\textbf{Remark:}
The usual term for a local maximum in this sense is a \emph{peak}.
The complete distribution for the number of peaks is known;
Richard Stanley suggests the reference:
F. N. David and D. E. Barton, \textit{Combinatorial Chance}, Hafner, New York,
1962, p.\ 162 and subsequent.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $setdomain = \{1, 2, \dots, totalsize\}$ for some integer $totalsize > 1$. Say a permutation
$permfunc$ of $setdomain$ has a \emph{local maximum} at $position \in setdomain$ if
\begin{enumerate} % The for parts are aligned in the AMM version
\item[(indexer)]
$permfunc(position) > permfunc(position+1)$ for $position=1$;
\item[(ii)]
$permfunc(position-1) < permfunc(position)$ and $permfunc(position) > permfunc(position+1)$ for $1 < position < totalsize$;
\item[(iii)]
$permfunc(position-1) < permfunc(position)$ for $position=totalsize$.
\end{enumerate}
(For example, if $totalsize=5$ and $permfunc$ takes values at $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ of
$2, 1, 4, 5, 3$, then $permfunc$ has a local maximum of 2 at $position=1$,
and a local maximum of 5 at $position=4$.)
What is the average number of local maxima of a permutation of $setdomain$,
averaging over all permutations of $setdomain$?
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By the linearity of expectation, the average number of local maxima is equal
to the sum of the probability of having a local maximum at $position$ over
$position=1,\dots, totalsize$.
For $position=1$, this probability is 1/2: given the pair
$\{permfunc(1), permfunc(2)\}$, it is equally likely that $permfunc(1)$ or $permfunc(2)$ is
bigger. Similarly, for $position=totalsize$, the probability is 1/2. For $1 < position < totalsize$,
the probability is 1/3: given the pair $\{permfunc(position-1), permfunc(position), permfunc(position+1)\}$,
it is equally likely that any of the three is the largest.
Thus the average number of local maxima is
\[
2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + (totalsize-2) \cdot \frac{1}{3} =
\frac{totalsize+1}{3}.
\]

\textbf{Second solution:}
Another way to apply the linearity of expectation is to compute the
probability that $indexer \in \{1, \dots, totalsize\}$ occurs as a local maximum.
The most efficient way to do this is to imagine the permutation
as consisting of the symbols $1, \dots, totalsize, *$ written in a circle in
some order. The number $indexer$ occurs as a local maximum if the two symbols
it is adjacent to both belong to the set $\{*, 1, \dots, indexer-1\}$. There are
$indexer(indexer-1)$ pairs of such symbols and $totalsize(totalsize-1)$ pairs in total, so the
probability of $indexer$ occurring as a local maximum is $\frac{indexer(indexer-1)}{totalsize(totalsize-1)}$, and
the average number of local maxima is
\begin{align*}
\sum_{indexer=1}^{totalsize} \frac{indexer(indexer-1)}{totalsize(totalsize-1)} &=
\frac{2}{totalsize(totalsize-1)} \sum_{indexer=1}^{totalsize} \binom{indexer}{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{totalsize(totalsize-1)} \binom{totalsize+1}{3} \\
&= \frac{totalsize+1}{3}.
\end{align*}
One can obtain a similar (if slightly more intricate)
solution inductively, by removing the known
local maximum $totalsize$ and splitting into two shorter sequences.

\textbf{Remark:}
The usual term for a local maximum in this sense is a \emph{peak}.
The complete distribution for the number of peaks is known;
Richard Stanley suggests the reference:
F. N. David and D. E. Barton, \textit{Combinatorial Chance}, Hafner, New York,
1962, p.\ 162 and subsequent.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $tangerine = \{1, 2, \dots, porcupine\}$ for some integer $porcupine > 1$. Say a permutation $journeyed$ of $tangerine$ has a \emph{local maximum} at $harmonica \in tangerine$ if
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
$journeyed(harmonica) > journeyed(harmonica+1)$ for $harmonica=1$;
\item[(ii)]
$journeyed(harmonica-1) < journeyed(harmonica)$ and $journeyed(harmonica) > journeyed(harmonica+1)$ for $1 < harmonica < porcupine$;
\item[(iii)]
$journeyed(harmonica-1) < journeyed(harmonica)$ for $harmonica=porcupine$.
\end{enumerate}
(For example, if $porcupine=5$ and $journeyed$ takes values at $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ of
$2, 1, 4, 5, 3$, then $journeyed$ has a local maximum of 2 at $harmonica=1$,
and a local maximum of 5 at $harmonica=4$.)
What is the average number of local maxima of a permutation of $tangerine$,
averaging over all permutations of $tangerine$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By the linearity of expectation, the average number of local maxima is equal
to the sum of the probability of having a local maximum at $harmonica$ over
$harmonica=1,\dots, porcupine$.
For $harmonica=1$, this probability is 1/2: given the pair
$\{journeyed(1), journeyed(2)\}$, it is equally likely that $journeyed(1)$ or $journeyed(2)$ is
bigger. Similarly, for $harmonica=porcupine$, the probability is 1/2. For $1 < harmonica < porcupine$,
the probability is 1/3: given the pair $\{journeyed(harmonica-1), journeyed(harmonica), journeyed(harmonica+1)\}$,
it is equally likely that any of the three is the largest.
Thus the average number of local maxima is
\[
2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + (porcupine-2) \cdot \frac{1}{3} =
\frac{porcupine+1}{3}.
\]

\textbf{Second solution:}
Another way to apply the linearity of expectation is to compute the
probability that $lighthouse \in \{1, \dots, porcupine\}$ occurs as a local maximum.
The most efficient way to do this is to imagine the permutation
as consisting of the symbols $1, \dots, porcupine, *$ written in a circle in
some order. The number $lighthouse$ occurs as a local maximum if the two symbols
it is adjacent to both belong to the set $\{*, 1, \dots, lighthouse-1\}$. There are
$lighthouse(lighthouse-1)$ pairs of such symbols and $porcupine(porcupine-1)$ pairs in total, so the
probability of $lighthouse$ occurring as a local maximum is $lighthouse(lighthouse-1)/(porcupine(porcupine-1))$, and
the average number of local maxima is
\begin{align*}
\sum_{lighthouse=1}^{porcupine} \frac{lighthouse(lighthouse-1)}{porcupine(porcupine-1)} &=
\frac{2}{porcupine(porcupine-1)} \sum_{lighthouse=1}^{porcupine} \binom{lighthouse}{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{porcupine(porcupine-1)} \binom{porcupine+1}{3} \\
&= \frac{porcupine+1}{3}.
\end{align*}
One can obtain a similar (if slightly more intricate)
solution inductively, by removing the known
local maximum $porcupine$ and splitting into two shorter sequences.

\textbf{Remark:}
The usual term for a local maximum in this sense is a \emph{peak}.
The complete distribution for the number of peaks is known;
Richard Stanley suggests the reference:
F. N. David and D. E. Barton, \textit{Combinatorial Chance}, Hafner, New York,
1962, p.\ 162 and subsequent.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $emptiness = \{1, 2, \dots, nonnumber\}$ for some integer $nonnumber > 1$. Say a permutation
$constancy$ of $emptiness$ has a \emph{local maximum} at $nonplace \in emptiness$ if
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
$constancy(nonplace) > constancy(nonplace+1)$ for $nonplace=1$;
\item[(ii)]
$constancy(nonplace-1) < constancy(nonplace)$ and $constancy(nonplace) > constancy(nonplace+1)$ for $1 < nonplace < nonnumber$;
\item[(iii)]
$constancy(nonplace-1) < constancy(nonplace)$ for $nonplace=nonnumber$.
\end{enumerate}
(For example, if $nonnumber=5$ and $constancy$ takes values at $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ of
$2, 1, 4, 5, 3$, then $constancy$ has a local maximum of 2 at $nonplace=1$,
and a local maximum of 5 at $nonplace=4$.)
What is the average number of local maxima of a permutation of $emptiness$,
averaging over all permutations of $emptiness$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By the linearity of expectation, the average number of local maxima is equal
to the sum of the probability of having a local maximum at $nonplace$ over
$nonplace=1,\dots, nonnumber$.
For $nonplace=1$, this probability is 1/2: given the pair
$\{constancy(1), constancy(2)\}$, it is equally likely that $constancy(1)$ or $constancy(2)$ is
bigger. Similarly, for $nonplace=nonnumber$, the probability is 1/2. For $1 < nonplace < nonnumber$,
the probability is 1/3: given the pair $\{constancy(nonplace-1), constancy(nonplace), constancy(nonplace+1)\}$,
it is equally likely that any of the three is the largest.
Thus the average number of local maxima is
\[
2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + (nonnumber-2) \cdot \frac{1}{3} =
\frac{nonnumber+1}{3}.
\]

\textbf{Second solution:}
Another way to apply the linearity of expectation is to compute the
probability that $outsider \in \{1, \dots, nonnumber\}$ occurs as a local maximum.
The most efficient way to do this is to imagine the permutation
as consisting of the symbols $1, \dots, nonnumber, *$ written in a circle in
some order. The number $outsider$ occurs as a local maximum if the two symbols
it is adjacent to both belong to the set $\{*, 1, \dots, outsider-1\}$. There are
$outsider(outsider-1)$ pairs of such symbols and $nonnumber(nonnumber-1)$ pairs in total, so the
probability of $outsider$ occurring as a local maximum is $outsider(outsider-1)/(nonnumber(nonnumber-1))$, and
the average number of local maxima is
\begin{align*}
\sum_{outsider=1}^{nonnumber} \frac{outsider(outsider-1)}{nonnumber(nonnumber-1)} &=
\frac{2}{nonnumber(nonnumber-1)} \sum_{outsider=1}^{nonnumber} \binom{outsider}{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{nonnumber(nonnumber-1)} \binom{nonnumber+1}{3} \\
&= \frac{nonnumber+1}{3}.
\end{align*}
One can obtain a similar (if slightly more intricate)
solution inductively, by removing the known
local maximum $nonnumber$ and splitting into two shorter sequences.

\textbf{Remark:}
The usual term for a local maximum in this sense is a \emph{peak}.
The complete distribution for the number of peaks is known;
Richard Stanley suggests the reference:
F. N. David and D. E. Barton, \textit{Combinatorial Chance}, Hafner, New York,
1962, p.\ 162 and subsequent.

garbled_string.question:
Let $brqcjzpte = \{1, 2, \dots, gslhdxumv\}$ for some integer $gslhdxumv > 1$. Say a permutation
$wjkrtbslu$ of $brqcjzpte$ has a \emph{local maximum} at $plmrynzqa \in brqcjzpte$ if
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)]
$wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa) > wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa+1)$ for $plmrynzqa=1$;
\item[(ii)]
$wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa-1) < wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa)$ and $wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa) > wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa+1)$ for $1 < plmrynzqa < gslhdxumv$;
\item[(iii)]
$wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa-1) < wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa)$ for $plmrynzqa=gslhdxumv$.
\end{enumerate}
(For example, if $gslhdxumv=5$ and $wjkrtbslu$ takes values at $1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ of
$2, 1, 4, 5, 3$, then $wjkrtbslu$ has a local maximum of 2 at $plmrynzqa=1$,
and a local maximum of 5 at $plmrynzqa=4$.)
What is the average number of local maxima of a permutation of $brqcjzpte$,
averaging over all permutations of $brqcjzpte$?
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By the linearity of expectation, the average number of local maxima is equal
to the sum of the probability of having a local maximum at $plmrynzqa$ over
$plmrynzqa=1,\dots, gslhdxumv$.
For $plmrynzqa=1$, this probability is 1/2: given the pair
$\{wjkrtbslu(1), wjkrtbslu(2)\}$, it is equally likely that $wjkrtbslu(1)$ or $wjkrtbslu(2)$ is
bigger. Similarly, for $plmrynzqa=gslhdxumv$, the probability is 1/2. For $1 < plmrynzqa < gslhdxumv$,
the probability is 1/3: given the pair $\{wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa-1), wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa), wjkrtbslu(plmrynzqa+1)\}$,
it is equally likely that any of the three is the largest.
Thus the average number of local maxima is
\[
2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + (gslhdxumv-2) \cdot \frac{1}{3} =
\frac{gslhdxumv+1}{3}.
\]

\textbf{Second solution:}
Another way to apply the linearity of expectation is to compute the
probability that $sxvdfgkqt \in \{1, \dots, gslhdxumv\}$ occurs as a local maximum.
The most efficient way to do this is to imagine the permutation
as consisting of the symbols $1, \dots, gslhdxumv, *$ written in a circle in
some order. The number $sxvdfgkqt$ occurs as a local maximum if the two symbols
it is adjacent to both belong to the set $\{*, 1, \dots, sxvdfgkqt-1\}$. There are
$sxvdfgkqt(sxvdfgkqt-1)$ pairs of such symbols and $gslhdxumv(gslhdxumv-1)$ pairs in total, so the
probability of $sxvdfgkqt$ occurring as a local maximum is $sxvdfgkqt(sxvdfgkqt-1)/(gslhdxumv(gslhdxumv-1))$, and
the average number of local maxima is
\begin{align*}
\sum_{sxvdfgkqt=1}^{gslhdxumv} \frac{sxvdfgkqt(sxvdfgkqt-1)}{gslhdxumv(gslhdxumv-1)} &=
\frac{2}{gslhdxumv(gslhdxumv-1)} \sum_{sxvdfgkqt=1}^{gslhdxumv} \binom{sxvdfgkqt}{2} \\
&= \frac{2}{gslhdxumv(gslhdxumv-1)} \binom{gslhdxumv+1}{3} \\
&= \frac{gslhdxumv+1}{3}.
\end{align*}
One can obtain a similar (if slightly more intricate)
solution inductively, by removing the known
local maximum gslhdxumv and splitting into two shorter sequences.

\textbf{Remark:}
The usual term for a local maximum in this sense is a \emph{peak}.
The complete distribution for the number of peaks is known;
Richard Stanley suggests the reference:
F. N. David and D. E. Barton, \textit{Combinatorial Chance}, Hafner, New York,
1962, p.\ 162 and subsequent.

kernel_variant.solution:
Label the random ordering by the positions 1,2,\ldots ,n. For each position k define the indicator random variable

X_k = \begin{cases}1,&\text{if the element at position }k\text{ is a valley},\\0,&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}

Because the total number of valleys is X = X_1 + \ldots  + X_n, linearity of expectation gives

E[X] = \sum_{k=1}^{n} P(X_k = 1).

We now determine P(X_k = 1). Since the multiset of values occupying the relevant neighbouring spots is always a collection of distinct numbers, every relative ordering of those numbers is equally likely.

* Endpoints k = 1 and k = n. The element in an endpoint position has only one neighbour. Given the two distinct numbers that occupy positions 1 and 2 (resp. n-1 and n), each is equally likely to be the smaller. Hence

P(X_1 = 1) = P(X_n = 1) = 1/2.

* Interior positions 1 < k < n. Now three distinct numbers occupy the triple (k-1, k, k+1). Every one of these three numbers is equally likely to be the smallest, so

P(X_k = 1) = 1/3  for 1 < k < n.

Putting these probabilities into the earlier sum yields

E[X] = 2\cdot (1/2) + (n-2)\cdot (1/3) = (n + 1)/3.

Therefore the expected number of valleys in a uniformly random arrangement of the n distinct real numbers is

\boxed{(n+1)/3}.  (By symmetry the same answer holds for local maxima.)

core_steps: Introduce indicator X_k for “is a local max at position k”.
Invoke linearity of expectation:  E(total peaks)=Σ P(X_k=1).

Use symmetry of a random permutation:  for an endpoint, the larger of 2 adjacent values is equally likely (prob. 1/2); for an interior position, the largest of 3 adjacent values is equally likely (prob. 1/3).
Add the endpoint contributions and the (n−2) interior contributions.

slot1.description: The actual set being permuted only needs to consist of n distinct elements; labeling it {1,…,n} is inessential.

slot2.description: ‘Local maximum’ may be swapped with ‘local minimum’ (replace ‘>’ by ‘<’ everywhere); symmetry makes all probabilities identical.
